target.name: Lyten
target.founder: Dan Cook, Bharat Sastri, Mike Stuart

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $1.3B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Materials (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 1 same-sector peers exist in Materials, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Lyten is a Series C materials-science company founded by Dan Cook, Bharat Sastri, and Mike Stuart, having raised $1.33B to date. The comparable universe in Materials is sparse: two private peers (Vulcan Elements, Noveon Magnetics) exist, with no public comparables identified, and insufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage peers within Materials to support a reliable peer-median approach.

Given data constraints, the valuation relies on stage-progression methodology. Using $1.3B in total raised as a proxy for enterprise value, a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C progression yields an implied valuation of $2.3B. A sensitivity band bracketing execution risk places the range between $1.9B (low) and $2.6B (high), centered on the $2.3B median.

The primary limitation is the narrow and immature peer set in Materials at comparable stage and scale. The analysis does not triangulate across multiple independent methods, leaving valuation dependent on a single assumption about future funding round mechanics. Key uncertainties include Lyten's unit economics relative to the sparse peer group, market adoption timelines for its technology, and whether the 1.7x multiplier reflects realistic investor expectations for this sector and stage. Further tightening of the range would require either expanded peer data or forward-looking financial projections not present in available inputs.
